unit.title: Models of the particulate nature of matter

title: The nuclear atom
summary: The atom is the foundation of the whole course. This lesson builds the nuclear model from the ground up: the relative mass and charge of protons, neutrons and electrons; how atomic number and mass number define an atom; how to read and write nuclide symbols; what isotopes are and why they matter; how a mass spectrometer reveals isotopic abundance; and how to calculate relative atomic mass as a weighted average. It closes with a fully worked exam calculation marked exactly the way our engine marks your own.
learningObjectives: State the position, relative mass, and relative charge of protons, neutrons, and electrons.
Define atomic number (Z), mass number (A), and isotopes of an element, and write and interpret nuclide symbols.
Deduce the number of protons, neutrons, and electrons in atoms and ions from nuclide notation.
Explain how a mass spectrometer determines isotopic abundance, and calculate relative atomic mass from isotopic composition.
levelNote: Common to SL and HL at this level.

simpleExplanation.title: The Atom's Tiny Core
simpleExplanation.summary: Atoms are mostly empty space with a tiny, dense, positively charged nucleus at their centre, containing protons and neutrons. Negatively charged electrons occupy the vast space around it in energy levels.
simpleExplanation.analogy: Imagine a huge sports stadium. The nucleus is like a marble on the centre spot — incredibly small and dense, holding almost all the mass. The electrons are like a few flies buzzing around the whole stadium: tiny, fast, filling a vast volume but weighing almost nothing.
steps: Read the nuclide symbol, e.g. $^{12}_{6}C$: the top number is the mass number (A), the bottom is the atomic number (Z).
Protons = Z. Electrons in a neutral atom = Z as well.
Neutrons = A − Z (mass number minus atomic number).
For ions, adjust only the electrons: subtract for cations (positive), add for anions (negative). The nucleus never changes.

flashcards.front: Relative charge and mass of a proton
flashcards.back: Charge +1, relative mass 1. Located in the nucleus.

flashcards.front: Relative charge and mass of a neutron
flashcards.back: Charge 0, relative mass 1. Located in the nucleus.

flashcards.front: Relative charge and mass of an electron
flashcards.back: Charge −1, relative mass ~1/1836 (treated as negligible). Located outside the nucleus in energy levels.
The number of protons in the nucleus. It defines which element the atom is — change Z and you change the element.
The total number of protons plus neutrons (nucleons) in the nucleus. Always a whole number for a single atom.
Atoms of the same element (same number of protons) with different numbers of neutrons, and therefore different mass numbers.
A chlorine atom with mass number 37 and atomic number 17: 17 protons, 17 electrons, and 37 − 17 = 20 neutrons.

flashcards.front: Mass number (A) vs relative atomic mass (A_r)
flashcards.back: A is an integer count of protons + neutrons for a SINGLE isotope. A_r is the WEIGHTED AVERAGE mass of all an element's isotopes — usually not a whole number, and found on the periodic table.

flashcards.front: Electrons in a negative ion, e.g. S²⁻
flashcards.back: Sulfur's atomic number is 16 (16 protons, 16 electrons when neutral). A 2− charge means 2 electrons gained: 16 + 2 = 18 electrons.

flashcards.front: What do isotopes of an element share?
flashcards.back: Identical chemical properties (same protons and electrons, so same electron arrangement). Physical properties such as density and rate of diffusion differ slightly because of the mass difference.

flashcards.front: What does a mass spectrometer measure?
flashcards.back: The mass-to-charge ratio (m/z) of ions and the relative abundance of each. For a single element it gives one peak per isotope; peak position = isotopic mass, peak height = abundance.
A_r = Σ(isotopic mass × fractional abundance). Convert every percentage to a decimal first, then multiply and add.

quickCheck.prompt: Two atoms are isotopes of the same element. They MUST differ in their number of:
Chlorine's relative atomic mass is 35.45, yet no single chlorine atom has that mass. This is because 35.45 is:
options: The mass number of the most common isotope
A weighted average of the isotope masses
A rounding error in the periodic table
The mass of a chlorine molecule Cl₂
quickCheck.answer: A weighted average of the isotope masses

quickCheck.prompt: Which set of relative charges is correct for proton, neutron, and electron in that order?
In a mass spectrum of a pure element, the HEIGHT of each peak tells you the:
How many neutrons are in the ion ⁴⁰₂₀Ca²⁺?

faq.q: Why is the mass of the electron ignored in the mass number?
faq.a: A proton or neutron is about 1836 times heavier than an electron, so the electrons contribute almost nothing to the atom's mass. Because the mass is concentrated in the nucleus, the mass number (A) counts only protons and neutrons.

faq.q: Can two different elements have the same mass number?
faq.a: Yes — these are called isobars. Argon-40 ($^{40}_{18}Ar$) has 18 protons and 22 neutrons; calcium-40 ($^{40}_{20}Ca$) has 20 protons and 20 neutrons. Both have mass number 40, but different numbers of protons make them different elements.

Chemically, no. Reactions depend on electron arrangement, which depends on the number of protons — identical across isotopes of one element. Physical properties such as density, diffusion rate and boiling point can differ slightly because the atoms have different masses.

faq.q: How does a mass spectrometer actually give abundance?
faq.a: A sample is vaporised and ionised, the ions are accelerated and separated by their mass-to-charge ratio, and a detector counts how many ions of each mass arrive. Each isotope produces a separate peak: its position gives the isotopic mass and its height gives the relative abundance — the exact numbers needed to calculate A_r.

sections.content: Modern chemistry is built on the atom. The ancient Greeks imagined an indivisible particle, but only in the 20th century — most famously through Ernest Rutherford's gold-foil experiment — did the atom's internal structure emerge: a tiny, dense, positively charged nucleus surrounded by electrons. This lesson unpacks the subatomic particles, shows how their numbers define an element and its isotopes, and builds to the calculation examiners test most often: finding relative atomic mass from isotopic abundance.
Subatomic particles

Atoms are built from three particles: protons, neutrons and electrons. Protons and neutrons, together called nucleons, sit in the small, dense, positively charged nucleus. Electrons occupy the much larger region of space around the nucleus, arranged in energy levels (shells).

items: **Proton (p⁺):** relative charge +1, relative mass 1 — in the nucleus.
**Neutron (n⁰):** relative charge 0, relative mass 1 — in the nucleus.
**Electron (e⁻):** relative charge −1, relative mass ~1/1836 (negligible) — outside the nucleus.
The nucleus holds almost all the mass but fills a tiny fraction of the atom's volume — the atom is mostly empty space.
A neutral atom has equal numbers of protons and electrons, so the positive and negative charges cancel.

sections.content: Nuclide notation, atomic number and mass number
To describe a specific atom precisely we use nuclide notation, which states the atomic number and mass number and so fixes the count of every particle.
Nuclide notation: $^{A}_{Z}X$

Here $X$ is the element symbol, $A$ is the **mass number** (protons + neutrons), and $Z$ is the **atomic number** (protons). Because the atomic number defines the element, $Z$ is often left off — the symbol alone tells you it. From these two numbers: protons = Z, neutrons = A − Z, and electrons = Z in a neutral atom (adjusted for any ionic charge).

sections.question: Deduce the number of protons, neutrons, and electrons in a neutral atom of fluorine, $^{19}_{9}F$, and in the fluoride ion, $F^{-}$.
sections.solution:
**For the neutral atom, $^{19}_{9}F$:**
*   **Protons:** the atomic number (Z, subscript) is 9, so 9 protons. [1]
*   **Electrons:** the atom is neutral, so electrons = protons = 9 electrons. [1]
*   **Neutrons:** the mass number (A, superscript) is 19, so neutrons = A − Z = 19 − 9 = 10 neutrons. [1]

**For the fluoride ion, $F^{-}$:**
*   **Protons and neutrons:** the nucleus is unchanged when an ion forms — still 9 protons and 10 neutrons. [1]
*   **Electrons:** a 1− charge means one electron gained: 9 + 1 = 10 electrons. [1]

Key idea: forming an ion changes only the electron count, never the nucleus.

sections.content: The nuclear model of the atom

Rutherford's experiment fired positive alpha particles at thin gold foil. Most passed straight through, showing the atom is mostly empty space, but a few bounced sharply back — evidence of a tiny, dense, positively charged nucleus. This is the nuclear model: protons and neutrons packed into a central nucleus, with electrons occupying the surrounding space in energy levels. It explains why the atom is electrically neutral overall and why nearly all its mass is concentrated in an almost vanishingly small volume.

Explore this model directly in the PhET “Build an Atom” simulation embedded below: add protons, neutrons and electrons and watch the element, charge and mass update live. Notice that adding a proton changes the element, while adding a neutron only changes the isotope.
Isotopes and relative atomic mass (A_r)

All atoms of an element share the same number of protons, but they can hold different numbers of neutrons. These variants are isotopes. For example, carbon-12 ($^{12}C$) has 6 protons and 6 neutrons, while carbon-14 ($^{14}C$) has 6 protons and 8 neutrons. Isotopes react identically because they have the same electron arrangement; only their masses differ. Since most elements occur as a mixture of isotopes, we describe an element's mass with a weighted average — the relative atomic mass ($A_r$) — rather than the mass of any single atom.
A_r = \sum (\text{isotopic mass} \times \text{fractional abundance})

A very common mistake is to confuse the mass number (A) with the relative atomic mass ($A_r$). 'A' is a whole-number count of protons + neutrons for a single isotope; '$A_r$' is the weighted average for the element, found on the periodic table, and is rarely a whole number. Never use the periodic-table $A_r$ to work out the number of neutrons.
How mass spectrometry gives isotopic abundance

A mass spectrometer is the instrument that supplies the two numbers the $A_r$ calculation needs. In outline, a sample is vaporised and ionised, the resulting ions are accelerated and then separated according to their mass-to-charge ratio (m/z), and a detector records how many ions of each mass arrive. For a pure element, every isotope produces its own peak.

items: The **horizontal position** of each peak (m/z) gives the **isotopic mass** — assuming singly charged ions, m/z equals the isotope's mass.
The **height** (or area) of each peak gives that isotope's **relative abundance**.
Converting each peak height to a percentage of the total gives the fractional abundances that feed straight into $A_r = \sum(\text{mass} \times \text{fractional abundance})$.
A spectrum with two chlorine peaks — a tall one at m/z 35 and a shorter one at m/z 37 — is exactly the data behind chlorine's $A_r$ of 35.45.

sections.content: So mass spectrometry and the $A_r$ calculation are two halves of one idea: the instrument measures which isotopes are present and in what proportion, and the weighted average turns that raw abundance data into the single relative atomic mass we use in chemistry.
Worked example — a model answer marked by our engine

sections.question: Chlorine exists as two isotopes: 75.77% Cl-35 and 24.23% Cl-37. Calculate the relative atomic mass of chlorine to 2 decimal places. [2]
sections.solution:
**Model answer (full working):**

1. **Convert percentages to fractional abundances:**
   *   Cl-35: 75.77% = 0.7577
   *   Cl-37: 24.23% = 0.2423

2. **Set up the weighted average** (method):
   $A_r = (0.7577 \times 35) + (0.2423 \times 37)$ **[M1]**

3. **Evaluate:**
   $A_r = 26.5195 + 8.9651 = 35.4846$

4. **Round to 2 decimal places:**
   $A_r = 35.48$ **[A1]**

---
**How our marking engine awards the marks:**
*   **M1 — method mark:** awarded for the correct weighted-average set-up, $(0.7577 \times 35) + (0.2423 \times 37)$. This mark is for *how* you combine mass and abundance, not for the final digits.
*   **A1 — answer mark:** awarded for the correct value, **35.48** (values that round correctly to 35.48 are accepted).
*   **ECF (error carried forward):** if you set the method up correctly but slip in the arithmetic, you still earn **M1** — the method mark protects you. Only the A1 is lost.
*   **Bare answers:** because this is a calculation, a **bare correct answer (35.48) with no working earns full marks**. But a **wrong answer with no working earns nothing** — there is no method on the page for the engine to reward. Always show the weighted-average line so M1 is safe even if the number is off.

(If the exam supplies precise isotopic masses of 34.97 and 36.97 u, the same method gives 35.45 u, the periodic-table value — the whole-number masses used above give 35.48, which the mark scheme accepts for this question.)

sections.content: The Practice button marks your own working exactly this way. Type your calculation, submit it, and the engine looks first for the weighted-average method (M1) and then for the correct value (A1). That means the method mark protects you even if your final number is slightly off — so always write the $(\text{abundance} \times \text{mass})$ line before the answer.
Second worked example — abundance from A_r

sections.question: Boron has two isotopes, B-10 and B-11, and a relative atomic mass of 10.81. Calculate the percentage abundance of each isotope.
sections.solution:
Let the fractional abundance of B-11 be $x$; then B-10 is $(1 - x)$.

1. **Set up the weighted average and put it equal to $A_r$:**
   $11x + 10(1 - x) = 10.81$ **[M1]**

2. **Expand and solve:**
   $11x + 10 - 10x = 10.81$
   $x = 0.81$

3. **Convert to percentages:**
   *   B-11: $0.81 = 81\%$
   *   B-10: $1 - 0.81 = 0.19 = 19\%$ **[A1]**

Check: $(0.19 \times 10) + (0.81 \times 11) = 1.90 + 8.91 = 10.81$ ✓. This reverses the standard calculation — the same weighted-average relationship, solved for the unknown abundance.

sections.content: Common mistakes examiners penalise

items: **Saying isotopes differ in protons or electrons** — isotopes of an element differ ONLY in neutrons. Different proton number means a different element entirely.

**Confusing mass number (A) with relative atomic mass ($A_r$)** — A is a whole number for one isotope; $A_r$ is the weighted average for the element and is usually not whole. Never use $A_r$ to count neutrons.
**Forgetting to convert percentages to decimals** — divide each abundance by 100 before multiplying, or divide the final sum by 100. Skipping this gives an answer 100× too large.
**Getting the charge of the particles wrong** — proton +1, neutron 0, electron −1; and adjusting the nucleus when an ion forms instead of only the electrons.
**Misreading a mass spectrum** — peak position (m/z) is the isotopic mass; peak height is the abundance. Reading them the wrong way round inverts the calculation.
**Rounding too early** — keep full precision through the working and round only the final $A_r$ to the decimal places asked for.

sections.content: Where this leads

The nuclear atom is not just an opening topic — it underpins electron configuration, the periodic table, bonding and every mole calculation to come. Relative atomic mass, built here from isotopic abundance, is the quantity you will use to convert between mass and amount of substance throughout the course. This content is common to SL and HL at this level.
